text: Hi, I'm Josh from AWS.
I'm Shehas Vamanjur from Prudential, and this is My Architecture.
So, Shehas, Prudential is a large financial institution,
and I imagine that with that territory comes lots of challenges around data,
you know, amounts of data and different types of data and how to deal with that.
Can you tell us a little bit more about those challenges
and how you dealt with them in this architecture?
Prudential, like any other modern enterprise,
needs to create its data products faster and cheaper.
And this architecture delivers on the promise of increased product creation velocity at lower cost.
So, I'm looking here at this user coming into this marketplace.
Can you tell us about that flow and why you chose this model?
The primary purpose of the marketplace is to create a browser-based experience
where the user can discover, understand, and access data.
This is the purpose of the marketplace application.
It simplifies the entire data community's experience with the data estate.
And this is done through a series of automations that move data across the different zones that you're seeing on the screen.
So, it comes in here.
I see some kind of manifest or document file here.
And then it's coming in through data sync.
Can you tell us about that flow and specifically why you chose data sync here or if there are other services?
Sure. The manifest contains instructions for the kind of data the user would like to move across the data estate.
For example, the data could exist in file systems, databases, APIs, streams, any number of different source systems in the enterprise.
Data sync is an example of an AWS service that we use to bring data from file systems.
There are other services that we currently do not represent on this rise, including Glue, which is connected to JDBC sources,
Kinesis, which is connected to streaming sources, DMS, which is connected to databases, sources, and so on.
But the manifest-driven experience abstracts the user from the nature of the source and the transport and shipping involved in moving data across this particular estate.
It sounds like it's really adaptable and flexible to a bunch of different data types, which is what we were talking about earlier with all those different data types and you see it in enterprises.
So we have S3 and Redshift in the next phase here in the standardization.
How does Glue DataBrew factor into that standardization story and why do you have S3 and Redshift?
So the purpose of this application is to support multipurpose usage of data.
S3 represents a lake pattern of usage which is connected to data science and machine learning.
Redshift is connected to a house pattern of architecture which is usually connected to business intelligence use cases.

And Glue DataBrew is our transformation tool which we use to dynamically create the automatic workflows to move data from the landing zone to a standard zone that represents the source in a time series manner.
This layer is also extensible with other technologies such as vector databases as well as graph databases.
So looking at all these services, you talked about the data lakes and all these standardization and refinement steps.
There's usually a lot of data engineers, human activity involved in this process, but you're looking to automate.
How much of this is currently automated today?
You point to an important concept that addresses both velocity as well as lower cost.
This portion of moving data from the source to the landing zone, which is step one, and to the standardized zone, which is step two, is fully automated.
So no human being writes code anywhere at Prudential to do this.
The subject of refinement of data, which is where business rules are written, this is where human engineers come into this picture.
And now they spend most of their time in this space.
Historically, they would have spent a significant portion of that 80% in moving the data across this estate.
Yeah, so now they can focus on what matters and add value here in the refinement stage.
I want to move to lake formation in this governance step here.
Lake formation, I imagine, is playing an important role in governing the rest of this workflow.
Can you tell me more about how you're using it?
Sure.
Now, one of the three things that the marketplace allows you to do is discover, find, and access.
The access is where lake formation comes into this picture.
Now, throughout this journey, what we've described is a shipping model and an organizational model for data that brings data to a usable form.
Now comes access to this data, which is a security and a governance question.
Lake formation is a service that we use to centrally secure access to S3 and Redshift.
Now, the marketplace similarly delivers access through the manifest, which is an instruction request for access, which then goes through approval workflows.
That is the purpose of using AWS Lake Formation here, to centrally govern all access privileges across this estate.
What I like here is that the user can specify what they want to achieve and not necessarily how it's going to be achieved under the hood.
Right? You can automate that away and you can have the Lake Formation manage the governance and you can have these automation steps hinder the standardization.
Yes. AWS services work very well under this abstraction layer of a marketplace application in order to orchestrate these flows minus the user having to know about them. That is correct.
And then lastly, we have this SageMaker and Athena consumer section here. Can you tell me a little bit about that?
This layer represents consumption models, which is both traditional as well as advanced analytics.
Amazon represents Athena represents a SQL interface. SageMaker is a machine learning interface.
You could introduce any third party tools as well into this picture.
So this is a very extensible and flexible architecture and thank you so much for sharing with us today.
Thanks for having me.
